g.data-narration: Welcome to Principles of Experimentation in Design. This topic covers fundamental methods for conducting rigorous scientific experiments.
Experimental design is crucial for research validity. In CS, it helps evaluate algorithms, test software performance, and ensure reliable results.
g.text: • Foundation for ANOVA and hypothesis testing

g.data-narration: The four pillars of experimental design are Replication, Randomization, Blinding, and Control Groups. Each plays a vital role.
g.text: Four Core Principles of Experimental Design

g.data-narration: Replication means performing the same experiment multiple times under identical conditions to ensure result consistency and reliability.
g.text: Definition: Performing the same test under

g.data-narration: Randomization assigns experimental units to treatment groups using random procedures, eliminating systematic bias in experiments.
Blinding prevents bias by keeping subjects or researchers unaware of group assignments. Single-blind hides from subjects; double-blind hides from both.
Control groups receive no treatment or standard treatment, providing a baseline for comparing experimental results against normal conditions.
Factorial designs study multiple factors simultaneously. One-way examines one factor; two-way examines two factors and their interactions.
g.text: Studies ONE factor at a time
Interaction Effect: How factors affect each other

g.data-narration: ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) is a statistical method that compares group means to determine if differences are statistically significant.
Let's apply these principles to a real CS scenario: comparing four sorting algorithms to find the best performer on real-world data.
g.text: Problem: Evaluate four sorting algorithms (A, B, C, D)
to determine which performs best on real-world datasets.

g.data-narration: We apply all four principles: run 50 iterations for replication, randomize dataset assignment, use blinding, and include QuickSort as control.
We use ANOVA to analyze results, testing if any algorithm performs significantly differently from others based on execution time.
After ANOVA finds significance, post-hoc tests like Tukey's HSD identify which specific algorithm pairs differ significantly from each other.
g.text: When ANOVA shows significance, conduct pairwise
Algorithm B significantly faster than A, C, D

g.data-narration: Key principles: Replication ensures reliability, randomization removes bias, blinding prevents expectation effects, and control groups provide comparison baselines.
These principles are essential for ANOVA, hypothesis testing, and research validity. Master them to excel in Module 5 and beyond.
g.text: These principles are prerequisites for: One-Way ANOVA, Two-Way ANOVA,
Hypothesis Testing, F-Test, and Confidence Intervals
